g.data-narration: Welcome to the Algorithm Analysis Framework. This module provides essential tools for evaluating algorithmic efficiency systematically.
g.text: BCS401 - Analysis and Design of Algorithms

g.data-narration: Algorithm analysis enables us to measure how performance scales with input size, making informed implementation decisions.
g.text: Consider searching a database with millions of records:
Linear search: seconds vs. Binary search: milliseconds
This framework provides mathematical tools to measure these differences precisely.

g.data-narration: Two foundational concepts: input size measures problem magnitude, and basic operations count correlates with running time.
g.text: Choosing the right metric is critical.

g.data-narration: Big-O notation provides an upper bound on running time, describing worst-case performance with O(g(n)) = O-notation.
g.text: f(n) = O(g(n)) if ∃ c, n₀: 0 ≤ f(n) ≤ c·g(n) for n ≥ n₀
• O(n²) = won't exceed some constant × n²
• Provides upper limit on running time
Example: If f(n) = 2n² + 3n + 1, then f(n) = O(n²)

g.data-narration: Big-Omega notation establishes a lower bound on running time, describing best-case performance with mathematical precision.
g.text: f(n) = Ω(g(n)) if ∃ c, n₀: 0 ≤ c·g(n) ≤ f(n) for n ≥ n₀
• Ω(n) = at least some constant × n
• Provides lower limit on running time
Example: If f(n) = 2n² + 3n + 1, then f(n) = Ω(n²)

g.data-narration: Theta notation provides tight bounds when upper and lower limits match, indicating exact growth rate of an algorithm.
g.text: f(n) = Θ(g(n)) if ∃ c₁,c₂,n₀: c₁·g(n) ≤ f(n) ≤ c₂·g(n) for n ≥ n₀
• Both upper AND lower bounds match

g.data-narration: Algorithms are classified by time complexity from constant O(1) through factorial O(n!), with each class representing different growth rates.
This graph illustrates how different complexity classes grow as input size increases, from constant to factorial growth.
Algorithm performance varies with input: best case is optimal input, worst case is pathological input, average case requires probability assumptions.
g.text: Best, Worst, and Average Case Analysis
Linear search: target at first → O(1)

g.data-narration: Space complexity measures memory requirements as function of input size, including both input data and auxiliary space for computations.
g.text: O(1) auxiliary space - uses constant extra memory
O(n) or more - requires significant extra memory
Example: Merge sort, Recursion call stack

g.data-narration: Example 1: A simple loop running n times performs O(n) or linear time complexity with one basic operation per iteration.
g.text: Loop executes n times × 1 addition per iteration
Time Complexity: Θ(n) or O(n) - Linear Time

g.data-narration: Example 2: Nested loops with variable inner range create triangular iteration pattern, resulting in quadratic O(n²) complexity.
Example 3: Recursive factorial algorithm has linear time complexity and space complexity due to n recursive call stack levels.
g.text: Recursion depth: n calls | One multiplication per call
Time: Θ(n) | Space: Θ(n) for call stack

g.data-narration: Key takeaways: Use O for worst-case bounds, Ω for best-case, Θ for tight bounds. Focus on growth rates, drop constants, identify dominant terms.

g.text: Complexity Order: O(1) < O(log n) < O(n) < O(n log n) < O(n²) < O(2ⁿ)
Big-O = Upper bound (worst), Big-Ω = Lower bound (best), Θ = Tight bound
Drop constants & lower terms: O(2n+3) → O(n), dominant term wins
Prove f(n)=O(g(n)) by finding constants c and n₀ satisfying the definition
Analyze both time and space: O(1) auxiliary = in-place algorithm
